items.question_text: A local controller is using a certified tower radar display. The controller wants to help a VFR aircraft navigate toward the airport. Which of the following may the controller lawfully do?
items.explanation: A certified tower radar display may be used to provide suggested headings or directions to VFR aircraft as an advisory aid to navigation. The phraseology uses 'PROCEED (direction)-BOUND' or 'SUGGESTED HEADING (degrees)' — these are suggestions, not radar vectors. The note emphasizes the pilot must understand these are advisory in nature.

choices.text: Issue a formal radar vector with a specific heading and altitude assignment.
Provide radar separation services between the VFR aircraft and other IFR traffic.
Provide a suggested heading as an advisory aid to navigation using 'SUGGESTED HEADING' phraseology.
Clear the aircraft for a radar approach to the active runway.

items.question_text: A controller responds to an initial call from an aircraft approaching Class D airspace with the aircraft's call sign followed by 'standby.' Before the controller can provide further service, a runway incursion requires immediate attention. What should the controller do regarding the aircraft waiting to enter?
items.explanation: Responding with the call sign and 'standby' establishes two-way communications, which legally permits the pilot to enter Class D airspace. If workload or traffic conditions then prevent immediate provision of services, the controller must instruct the pilot to REMAIN OUTSIDE DELTA AIRSPACE AND STANDBY. The 'standby' alone permits entry; the explicit 'remain outside' phraseology is needed to keep the aircraft out when services cannot be provided.

choices.text: Do nothing further — the initial 'standby' response already instructs the pilot to wait outside the airspace.
Tell the pilot to contact approach control for entry authorization because the tower is unavailable.
Issue 'REMAIN OUTSIDE DELTA AIRSPACE AND STANDBY' because workload now prevents immediate service provision.
The communications are already established, so the pilot may enter and the controller must fit the aircraft in.

items.question_text: Under what daytime condition must runway edge lights be operated for the runway in use?
items.explanation: Between sunrise and sunset, runway edge lights are turned on following the same operational pattern as the nighttime rule when surface visibility is less than 2 miles. The daytime extension means the lights go on before an aircraft taxies onto the runway (departures) or begins final approach/enters the surface area (arrivals), and stay on until the aircraft leaves the surface area. A common misconception is that runway edge lights are only a nighttime requirement.

choices.text: Only when requested by the pilot, regardless of the reported visibility.
When surface visibility is less than 2 miles, following the same pattern as nighttime operations.
Only when the ceiling is below 1,000 feet or visibility is below 3 miles, whichever occurs first.
Daytime operation of runway edge lights is never required; they are for nighttime use only.

items.question_text: How do the weather conditions required to clear the POFZ differ from those required to clear the final approach OCS?
items.explanation: The POFZ must be clear when the aircraft is within 2 miles of the threshold AND the ceiling is below 300 feet OR visibility is less than 3/4 SM. The final approach OCS must be clear when within 2 miles AND the ceiling is below 800 feet OR visibility is less than 2 SM. The POFZ has stricter weather criteria because it protects a smaller, closer-in volume for aircraft executing an immediate missed approach. The OCS protects a larger volume and triggers at less severe weather minimums.

choices.text: The POFZ uses stricter criteria (ceiling below 300 ft or visibility below 3/4 SM) than the OCS (ceiling below 800 ft or visibility below 2 SM).
Both surfaces use identical weather criteria: ceiling below 300 feet or visibility below 3/4 SM.
The OCS uses stricter criteria than the POFZ because it protects a larger volume.
The POFZ requires only the distance trigger (within 2 miles); the OCS requires only the weather trigger.

items.question_text: At what point does an aircraft cleared for a stop-and-go transition from arriving to departing status?
items.explanation: The paragraph establishes that each maneuver type has a distinct transition point. For a stop-and-go, the aircraft is considered an arriving aircraft until it makes a complete stop; thereafter it is a departing aircraft. For touch-and-go, the transition is at touchdown. For a low approach, it is at crossing the landing threshold. These transition points matter because they determine which separation rules apply.

choices.text: At the moment of touchdown on the runway.
When the aircraft comes to a complete stop.
When the aircraft begins its takeoff roll after the stop.
When the aircraft exits the runway after completing the maneuver.

items.question_text: A preceding aircraft has just landed on Runway 27 and is still rolling out. The controller can see the aircraft is decelerating rapidly and will be clear of the runway well before the next departure, which is holding in position, can begin its takeoff roll. May the controller issue the takeoff clearance now?
items.explanation: The anticipating separation rule permits issuing a takeoff clearance before prescribed separation exists when there is reasonable assurance it will exist at the time the aircraft starts takeoff roll. It does not relax the separation standard — it only permits issuing the clearance earlier than the moment separation is actually achieved.

choices.text: No — a takeoff clearance may never be issued while another aircraft is still on the runway.
Yes, but only if the arriving aircraft has already passed the runway midpoint.
Yes, if there is reasonable assurance that separation will exist when the departure starts its takeoff roll.
No — anticipating separation applies only to landing clearances, not takeoff clearances.

items.question_text: An arriving aircraft reports on a 3-mile final for Runway 18 but the controller cannot visually spot it from the tower. The controller has confirmed via other means that the runway is clear and separation is assured. What should the controller include when issuing the landing clearance?
items.explanation: When an arriving aircraft reports at a position where it should be visible but has not been visually observed, the controller must advise the aircraft as part of the landing clearance that it is not in sight and restate the landing runway. The prescribed phraseology is 'NOT IN SIGHT, RUNWAY (number) CLEARED TO LAND.'

choices.text: Issue a standard landing clearance without modification because the runway has been confirmed clear.
Withhold the landing clearance entirely until visual contact is established with the aircraft.
Instruct the aircraft to go around because visual observation is a prerequisite for any landing clearance.
Include 'NOT IN SIGHT' in the landing clearance and restate the landing runway number.

items.question_text: What is the controller's obligation when an aircraft appears to have committed a regulatory violation while in the traffic pattern?
items.explanation: The paragraph directs controllers not to withhold a landing clearance indefinitely even when it appears a regulatory violation has occurred. The apparent violation might result from an emergency situation. The controller's obligation is to assist the pilot to the extent possible rather than using the landing clearance as an enforcement tool.

choices.text: Withhold the landing clearance and instruct the aircraft to contact the facility by telephone after landing.
Report the violation to the FSDO and withhold services until the investigation is complete.
Do not withhold the landing clearance indefinitely — assist the pilot to the extent possible.
Issue the clearance only after the pilot acknowledges and explains the apparent violation.

items.question_text: A floatplane pilot asks a tower controller to apply the special sea-lane separation provisions from Section 12 for an operation on a river near the airport. The river is an uncontrolled waterway with no formal sea-lane designation. How should the controller respond?
items.explanation: The controlling prerequisite is explicit: Section 12 applies only where sea lanes are established and controlled. An uncontrolled body of water, even if used by floatplanes, does not trigger the section. The controller applies standard control procedures instead.

choices.text: Decline to apply sea-lane provisions because the river lacks an established and controlled sea lane.
Apply the sea-lane provisions as a courtesy since the pilot requested them for a water operation.
Temporarily designate the river as a sea lane for the duration of the pilot's operation.
Apply Section 12 provisions because all water operations by floatplanes automatically qualify as sea-lane operations.

items.instruction: Place the following actions in the correct order that a controller must take before issuing a takeoff clearance when an LOA-covered vehicle is operating in the RSA near the active runway.
items.question_text: Arrange in correct sequence: (1) Issue takeoff clearance to the departing aircraft. (2) Verify the runway is free of all known ground vehicles, equipment, and personnel. (3) Authorize the LOA vehicle to proceed as requested in the RSA. (4) Confirm the vehicle remains at or behind the runway edge and has not entered the runway surface.
items.explanation: The correct sequence: first authorize the RSA operation using PROCEED AS REQUESTED (the vehicle may operate up to the runway edge under the LOA), then verify the runway itself is clear of all vehicles and personnel (even LOA vehicles cannot be on the runway surface before takeoff), confirm the vehicle remains at the edge, and only then issue takeoff clearance. The LOA authorizes RSA operations but does not permit vehicles on the runway surface when a departure is about to begin.

items.instruction: For each capability below, select whether it is permitted on an uncertified tower display workstation, a certified tower radar display, both, or neither.
items.question_text: Classify: (A) Visually locating aircraft relative to known geographic points. (B) Providing radar traffic advisories. (C) Determining an aircraft's exact location or spatial relationship to other aircraft. (D) Issuing suggested headings as an advisory aid to navigation.

choices.text: A: uncertified only; B: certified only; C: certified only; D: certified only
A: both; B: both; C: certified only; D: certified only
A: uncertified only; B: neither; C: both; D: both
A: both; B: certified only; C: both; D: both

items.explanation: (A) The permitted use of uncertified displays is to assist in visually locating aircraft or determining spatial relationship to known geographic points. Certified displays can also do this, but 'uncertified only' is the minimum correct classification. (B) Radar traffic advisories may only be provided using certified displays. (C) Determining exact location or spatial relationships is explicitly listed as a certified display capability. (D) Suggested headings are a certified display function. Uncertified displays cannot provide (B), (C), or (D).

items.instruction: A pilot is approaching Class D airspace. Place the following controller actions in the correct operational sequence when traffic conditions initially permit services but then become saturated.
items.question_text: Arrange in correct sequence: (1) Controller determines workload now prevents immediate provision of services. (2) Pilot establishes initial radio contact with the tower. (3) Controller issues 'REMAIN OUTSIDE DELTA AIRSPACE AND STANDBY.' (4) Controller responds with aircraft call sign and 'standby.'
items.explanation: Correct sequence: (2) the pilot calls the tower first. (4) The controller responds with call sign + 'standby,' which establishes two-way communications and legally permits Class D entry. (1) The controller then realizes workload prevents immediate service. (3) At this point, the controller must issue the explicit 'REMAIN OUTSIDE' instruction because the earlier 'standby' alone permits entry but services cannot be provided.

items.instruction: For each airport condition, indicate whether the controller must include an additional specific detail beyond the basic condition description when issuing timely information.
items.question_text: Classify: (A) Volcanic ash on the airport surface. (B) Construction work adjacent to the movement area. (C) Braking conditions caused by ice. (D) Parked aircraft on the movement area.

choices.text: A: must state whether ash is wet or dry (if known); B, C, D: basic description suffices
A: basic description suffices; B: must state exact construction boundaries; C: must state ice depth; D: must state aircraft type
A and C: must include additional detail; B and D: basic description suffices
All four conditions require additional specific detail beyond the basic description

items.explanation: Only volcanic ash (item g) carries the unique requirement to state whether the ash is wet or dry if that information is known. Dry ash and wet ash create different operational hazards — dry ash may necessitate minimum use of reverse thrust, while wet ash may produce degraded braking. The other listed conditions carry no equivalent requirement.

items.instruction: For each situation, determine whether the emergency-lighting rule requires action.
items.question_text: Classify: (A) A pilot declares an emergency with a MAYDAY call and reports engine failure. (B) A controller overhears a pilot report serious mechanical trouble but no emergency has been declared. (C) A vehicle reports a minor fluid leak on a taxiway with no aircraft affected. (D) An aircraft reports it will need to make an emergency landing in approximately 10 minutes due to low fuel.

choices.text: A, B, and D require action; C does not
Only A requires action because a formal MAYDAY was declared
A, B, C, and D all require action under the emergency-lighting rule
A and D require action; B and C do not

items.explanation: The trigger is controller awareness that an emergency 'has or will occur' — a formal MAYDAY is not required. (A) A declared emergency clearly triggers action. (B) The controller's awareness of serious trouble that could become an emergency triggers the 'will occur' prong. (D) An aircraft reporting a pending emergency landing directly triggers the 'will occur' prong. (C) A minor fluid leak is an airport condition issue, not an emergency.

items.instruction: For each ASDE safety logic alert scenario, select the correct controller response.
items.question_text: Classify the required response: (A) An arrival aircraft still airborne before the landing threshold activates a warning alert. (B) An arrival aircraft activates a warning alert to a taxiway. (C) Two arrival aircraft simultaneously activate an alert. (D) A known formation flight crosses the landing threshold and activates a warning alert, with all other factors acceptable.

choices.text: A: issue go-around; B: issue go-around; C: take appropriate action per assessed situation; D: may disregard
A: issue go-around; B: issue go-around; C: issue go-around to both; D: issue go-around
A: may disregard if runway appears clear; B: issue go-around; C: issue go-around; D: may disregard
A: issue go-around; B: advise pilot to check alignment; C: issue go-around to lower aircraft; D: may disregard

items.explanation: When a pre-threshold arrival activates a warning alert (A), issue go-around — the intent is that the aircraft does not land on that approach. Same for taxiway-targeted alerts (B). For two-aircraft alerts (C), the controller assesses and takes appropriate action per the situation. For known formation flights crossing the threshold (D), the rule provides an explicit exception allowing the alert to be disregarded if all other factors are acceptable.

items.instruction: Select the taxi clearance that includes all required elements and proper construction.
items.question_text: An aircraft at the general aviation ramp needs to taxi to Runway 27 for departure, with an intermediate hold short of Runway 18. Which clearance is properly constructed?

choices.text: "Taxi to Runway Two-Seven, hold short of Runway One-Eight on the way."
"Runway Two-Seven, taxi via Alpha, cross Runway One-Eight at Taxiway Alpha, hold short of Runway Two-Seven."
"Cleared to Runway Two-Seven, taxi at your discretion."
"Runway Two-Seven, taxi via Alpha, you'll need to hold somewhere before Runway One-Eight."

items.explanation: A proper taxi clearance must include the runway assignment, the specific taxi route in concise terms, any required crossing instructions with the crossing point, and the hold-short point. The correct choice includes the runway (Runway 27), route (via Alpha), crossing instruction with location, and hold position. The distractors omit route specificity, use vague language, or lack required elements.

items.instruction: Match each maneuver to the point at which the aircraft transitions from arriving to departing status.
items.question_text: Match: (A) Touch-and-go — (1) touchdown; (B) Stop-and-go — (2) complete stop; (C) Low approach — (3) crossing the landing threshold. Which pairing is correct?
items.explanation: Touch-and-go: transition at touchdown (the aircraft touches and immediately goes to full power). Stop-and-go: transition at complete stop (must come to a full stop before takeoff). Low approach: transition at crossing the landing threshold (never touches down). These transition points determine which separation rules apply.

items.instruction: Place the following gate-hold procedure elements in the correct sequence of events.
items.question_text: Arrange in correct operational sequence: (1) ATC advises aircraft of engine start time. (2) Gate-hold procedures are initiated. (3) ATC advises aircraft to start engines. (4) ATC announces gate-hold procedures are in effect and provides contact frequency. (5) Gate-hold procedures are terminated. (6) Pilot reports ready to taxi.
items.explanation: Correct sequence: (2) gate-hold procedures go into effect; (4) ATC announces the procedures and provides the contact frequency per the prescribed phraseology; (1) ATC provides the expected engine start time; (3) at the appropriate time, ATC advises to start engines; (6) pilot reports ready to taxi; (5) when gate-hold ends, ATC announces procedures no longer in effect.

items.front: What three specific circumstances require ground control to notify or advise local control?
items.back: 1) Aircraft taxied to a runway other than one previously designated as active. 2) Aircraft taxied to an intersection for takeoff. 3) Before releasing an aircraft when runways or aircraft are not visible — advise the aircraft's location first.

items.front: What are the three authorized uses of a certified tower radar display by local controllers?
items.back: 1) Determine aircraft identification, exact location, or spatial relationship to other aircraft. 2) Provide radar traffic advisories. 3) Provide suggested headings or directions to VFR aircraft as an advisory aid to navigation (not radar vectors).

items.front: What does each ATC light signal mean for an aircraft in flight?
items.back: Steady green = cleared to land. Flashing green = return for landing. Steady red = give way, continue circling. Flashing red = airport unsafe, do not land. Alternating red/green = exercise extreme caution. Used when radio communications cannot be employed.

items.front: What additional detail must accompany a volcanic ash report in timely airport condition information?
items.back: State whether the ash is wet or dry, if known. Dry ash may require minimum use of reverse thrust; wet ash may degrade braking. No other condition in the timely-information list requires this specific physical-state descriptor.

items.front: What four actions must the controller take while Braking Action Advisories are in effect?
items.back: 1) Issue latest braking report to each arrival and departure. 2) If no report for the runway, issue 'NO BRAKING ACTION REPORTS RECEIVED.' 3) Advise Airport Operator of good-to-medium, medium, medium-to-poor, poor, or nil reports. 4) Solicit PIREPs of braking action.

items.front: What triggers the emergency airport lighting rule, and is a formal declaration required?
items.back: Controller awareness that an emergency has occurred OR will occur. No formal declaration (MAYDAY) is required. Reasonable anticipation of an emergency from observed conditions or pilot reports is sufficient.

items.front: When must runway edge lights be operated during daytime hours?
items.back: Between sunrise and sunset when surface visibility is less than 2 miles. Apply the same operational pattern as nighttime: turn on before aircraft taxies onto runway (departures) or begins final approach/enters surface area (arrivals), and keep on until aircraft leaves the surface area.

items.front: What weather minimums trigger POFZ clearance versus OCS clearance, and why do they differ?
items.back: POFZ: aircraft within 2 miles AND ceiling below 300 ft OR visibility below 3/4 SM. OCS: aircraft within 2 miles AND ceiling below 800 ft OR visibility below 2 SM. POFZ criteria are stricter because it protects a smaller, closer-in volume for immediate missed approach.

items.front: What is anticipating separation for takeoff clearances?
items.back: Issuing a takeoff clearance before prescribed separation exists, based on reasonable assurance that separation WILL exist when the aircraft starts its takeoff roll. It does not relax separation standards — only the timing of the clearance.

items.front: What phraseology must accompany a landing clearance when the controller cannot visually spot a reporting aircraft?
items.back: "NOT IN SIGHT, RUNWAY (number) CLEARED TO LAND." This tells the pilot the controller lacks visual contact. The clearance may still be issued if separation is assured through other means.

items.front: May a controller withhold a landing clearance indefinitely to address an apparent regulatory violation?
items.back: No. The apparent violation may stem from an emergency. The controller must assist the pilot. The landing clearance must not be used as an enforcement tool.

items.front: What is the prerequisite for applying Section 12 sea-lane provisions?
items.back: Sea lanes must be both established AND controlled. Uncontrolled waterways do not trigger the section, even if floatplanes use them. The provisions apply only where formal sea-lane infrastructure exists.
